content:
Instead of a Bayonet — Segueing to Third Person here suggests the condensing of the “I” into the “We,” which I assume the intention of training is meant to achieve (recoding). When the “They” becomes the “He” an I is born. We realize you still exist once more when you’re by yourself, even if you all are calling your moms from the same phone. “Mom, don’t confuse me with the others.”

Calls of Duty — Logos is the commanding principle of this piece; an authorial voice enters in the parentheticals. If the style of this piece is replicated, I could see it becoming a journalistic voice (perhaps, informative voice) not in the style of a news-person, but with room to insert subjectivity.

On the Rifle Range — I had trouble tracking the action and location of characters, but was able to get a clearer picture on what was going down after multiple reads. The image of a wound and a scream (or an open and silent mouth) works really well, and the series of images lands when you shift from night whispers —> night whistles by the end of the piece (the trauma slipping into the ranks, or the fear of what’s to come). A large aspect of this collection aims “to exhume concepts of identity and anonymity through an extended exploration of unknowns,” so I understand the technical intention to avoid proper nouns…however, this is the first piece that tripped me up with the he, him, etc. a bit.
context: From document: Tomb of the Unknowns Notes — chunk 2 (chunk 2/4)
